expanded_description: Geometric constructions use only a compass and straightedge (an unmarked ruler) to create precise geometric figures. Copying a segment and copying an angle are the two most fundamental constructions—they transfer exact measurements without a ruler or protractor. These skills build the foundation for all more complex constructions and deepen understanding of geometric properties.

core_concept.principle: A geometric construction creates a figure using only two tools: a compass for drawing circles and arcs, and a straightedge for drawing straight lines. No measuring is allowed—the compass preserves distances, and the straightedge ensures straightness. Copying a segment transfers an exact length; copying an angle transfers an exact angle measure, both using only these classical Euclidean tools.
core_concept.explanation: Think of a compass as a distance-capturing device. When you place its points on a segment's endpoints, the compass 'remembers' that length. You can then walk that distance anywhere and mark it off. For angles, the compass captures the 'opening' by marking an arc across both sides, then recreating that same arc pattern at a new location. This is geometry in its purest form—no numbers, no rulers, no protractors, just circles and lines.

image.alt: Two panels: (1) A compass capturing the length of segment AB and transferring it to a new location to create congruent segment CD. (2) A compass capturing the opening of an angle by drawing an arc, then recreating that arc at a new vertex to copy the angle. Straightedge shown for drawing lines.
image.caption: Geometric constructions: Copying a segment (left) and copying an angle (right) using only compass and straightedge.

sections.introduction: Classical geometric constructions use exactly two tools: a compass and a straightedge. These are not measuring tools—the compass captures and transfers distances, and the straightedge draws straight lines. Understanding what these tools can and cannot do is essential before beginning any construction.

introduction_image.alt: A compass shown with its two legs—one with a sharp point for anchoring, one with a pencil for drawing. A straightedge shown as an unmarked ruler with no markings or numbers. Labels: 'Compass: captures and transfers distances, draws circles and arcs. Straightedge: draws straight lines, no measuring.'
introduction_image.caption: The two classical tools: compass (captures distances) and straightedge (draws lines, never measures).

key_concept.definition: A compass is a tool with two legs—a sharp point to anchor at a center and a pencil point to draw circles and arcs. It is used to capture a distance (by placing the points on two given points) and transfer that distance elsewhere. A straightedge is an unmarked ruler used only for drawing straight lines through given points. It cannot be used to measure lengths.
key_concept.context: These restrictions come from Euclidean geometry's axiomatic approach: constructions prove what can be built from basic postulates. The compass implements the idea that a circle can be drawn with any center and any radius. The straightedge implements the idea that a unique line exists through any two points.

image.alt: Left: Compass with labels on its parts—anchor point, pencil point, hinge for adjusting radius. An arc being drawn. Right: Straightedge with emphasis 'No markings — draws lines only'. A line being drawn through two points. Bottom: 'What they do: Compass → circles, arcs, transfer distances. Straightedge → straight lines.'
image.caption: Compass: draws circles, captures distances. Straightedge: draws lines, no measuring allowed.

process_flow.process: Set Up and Anchor the Compass
process_flow.description: Place the sharp point of the compass at the reference point—either an endpoint of a segment to capture its length, or the vertex of an angle to draw an arc.
process_flow.mechanism: Press the anchor point firmly at the desired center or endpoint. Adjust the compass opening by moving the pencil leg. For copying a segment, place anchor and pencil on the two endpoints to capture the exact length. For copying an angle, anchor at the vertex and set the pencil to any convenient radius.
process_flow.result: The compass is set to the desired radius or has captured the desired length.
applications: To capture segment AB: anchor at A, adjust so pencil is exactly on B.
To draw an arc on an angle: anchor at vertex, set to any convenient opening.
The compass must be held at the top, not by the legs, to avoid changing the setting.

image.alt: Two images: (1) Compass points on endpoints A and B of a segment, capturing its length. (2) Compass anchored at vertex O of an angle, set to a radius, about to draw an arc across both sides. Hands shown holding the compass correctly by the handle.
image.caption: Step 1: Set the compass—capture a segment length or set a radius for an arc.

process_flow.description: Without changing the compass opening, move the compass to the new location. For a segment, anchor at the starting point of the new segment and draw an arc on the target line. For an angle, recreate the arc pattern at the new vertex.
process_flow.mechanism: Lift the compass carefully—do not change the opening. Place the anchor at the new point. For copying a segment, the new point is the starting endpoint of the target ray; draw an arc intersecting the ray. For copying an angle, draw the first arc on the original angle, then with the SAME radius draw an arc from the new vertex.
process_flow.result: The captured measurement is marked at the new location.
applications: Copying a segment: anchor at starting point of the new ray, swing an arc to intersect it.
Copying an angle: draw base arc on original, then matching arc from new vertex.
The compass opening must remain absolutely unchanged during transfer.

image.alt: Two panels: (1) Compass moved to a new ray, anchor at its endpoint, drawing an arc that intersects the ray—this marks the copied segment length. (2) Compass at original angle vertex drawing an arc across both sides, then same radius used at new vertex to draw a matching base arc.
image.caption: Step 2: Transfer the captured distance or arc radius to the new location without changing the compass.

process_flow.process: Complete the Construction with the Straightedge
process_flow.description: Use the straightedge to draw the final lines. For a copied segment, draw the segment from the starting point to the arc intersection. For a copied angle, measure the chord on the original arc with the compass, transfer that chord length to the new arc, and draw the second side through the intersection.
process_flow.mechanism: For segment copying: place the straightedge through the starting point and the arc intersection, draw the segment. For angle copying: capture the chord (distance between arc intersections on the original angle sides) with the compass, transfer that chord to the new arc to locate the second intersection, then draw the second side from the vertex through this point.
process_flow.result: A completed construction: a segment congruent to the original, or an angle congruent to the original.
applications: Copied segment: CD— ≅ AB—, constructed without measuring either length.
Copied angle: ∠XYZ ≅ ∠ABC, constructed without measuring either angle.
Both constructions prove congruence by identical compass-captured distances.

image.alt: Two completed constructions: (1) Segment CD drawn from starting point to arc intersection, congruent to original AB, matching tick marks. (2) Angle copied: original angle with arc and chord marked, new angle with matching arc and chord, second side drawn. Congruence marks shown.
image.caption: Step 3: Complete with straightedge—draw the segment or the second side of the angle.

image.alt: A unified diagram showing the three-step process for both constructions side by side: (1) Set compass, (2) Transfer, (3) Complete with straightedge. Each step illustrated for both segment copying and angle copying. Clean, instructional layout.
image.caption: Construction process overview: Set, transfer, and complete—for both segment and angle copying.

sections.introduction: Copying a segment is the simplest geometric construction. Given a segment AB and a ray starting at point C, you construct a segment on that ray that is congruent to AB. This construction proves that lengths can be transferred precisely without measurement.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing: given segment AB (any length) and a ray starting at point C extending to the right. An arrow points to the completed construction: segment CD on that ray, congruent to AB, with matching tick marks. The compass is shown capturing length AB.
introduction_image.caption: Given: segment AB and ray from C. Goal: construct segment CD on the ray such that CD ≅ AB.

key_concept.definition: To copy a segment, capture its length with the compass, transfer that length to a new ray, and mark the endpoint. If C is the endpoint of a target ray, the constructed point D lies on the ray such that CD = AB. The result is CD— ≅ AB—.
key_concept.context: This construction is the basis for adding and subtracting segment lengths, constructing triangles given three sides (SSS), and creating geometric designs with precise repeated lengths. It is the simplest demonstration of the compass's distance-capturing power.

image.alt: Step-by-step construction: (1) Draw a ray from C. (2) Set compass to length AB. (3) With anchor at C, draw arc intersecting the ray at D. (4) Draw segment CD. Result: CD = AB, segments marked congruent with tick marks.
image.caption: Copying a segment: capture, transfer, mark. Result: CD ≅ AB.

classifications.value: A four-step process using compass and straightedge to construct a segment congruent to a given segment.
characteristics: Step 1: Draw a ray from the starting point C (the new endpoint) using the straightedge
Step 2: Place compass anchor on A and pencil on B to capture length AB
Step 3: Without changing the compass, move anchor to C and draw an arc intersecting the ray — this intersection is point D
Step 4: CD is the copied segment, and CD ≅ AB
classifications.behavior: The compass acts as a distance recorder. The radius setting equals the original segment length. The arc marks all points at that exact distance from C; the intersection with the ray gives the unique point D on that ray at distance AB from C.

examples.explanation:
Given: Segment AB (any length), and a point C on a line or ray.

1) Use straightedge to draw a ray from C in any direction.

2) Place compass points on A and B — this captures AB.

3) Move compass anchor to C, draw an arc crossing the ray — label intersection D.

4) CD ≅ AB. Verified: both measured by the exact same compass opening.

image.alt: Four sequential diagrams: (1) Given AB and ray from C. (2) Compass capturing AB. (3) Arc from C intersecting ray at D. (4) Completed segment CD with congruence marks matching AB.
image.caption: The four steps of copying a segment: draw ray, capture length, transfer, mark congruence.

image.alt: Complete construction diagram: Original segment AB with tick marks. Ray from C with constructed point D. Arc shown from C to D. Both segments highlighted and labeled congruent. Compass setting illustrated with dashed arc.
image.caption: Segment Copy Construction: CD ≅ AB, constructed without measuring either length.

sections.introduction: Copying an angle transfers the exact opening of an angle to a new location. Given ∠ABC and a ray starting at point E, you construct an angle with vertex E and the given ray as one side, such that the new angle is congruent to ∠ABC.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing: given ∠ABC (vertex B, sides BA and BC) and a ray starting at point E extending to the right. An arrow points to the completed construction: ∠FEG with vertex E, side EF along the given ray, and side EG constructed so ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC. Congruence arcs marked.
introduction_image.caption: Given: ∠ABC and ray from E. Goal: construct ∠FEG on that ray such that ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC.

key_concept.definition: To copy an angle, draw an arc across both sides of the original angle, capture the chord length between the arc intersections, transfer both the arc and chord to a new vertex, and draw the second side through the transferred intersection point. The resulting angle is congruent to the original.
key_concept.context: Copying an angle is essential for constructing parallel lines (by copying corresponding angles), constructing triangles given two sides and the included angle (SAS), and creating regular polygons. It is one of the most-used constructions in Euclidean geometry.

image.alt: Step-by-step construction: (1) Draw arc across original angle sides (intersections X and Y). (2) Draw same-radius arc from new vertex E (intersects ray at F). (3) Measure chord XY with compass. (4) Transfer chord from F to arc, marking G. (5) Draw side EG. Result: ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC.
image.caption: Copying an angle: capture the arc, measure the chord, transfer both. Result: ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC.

classifications.value: A five-step process using compass and straightedge to construct an angle congruent to a given angle.
characteristics: Step 1: From vertex B, draw an arc intersecting both sides of ∠ABC at points X and Y
Step 2: Without changing compass, anchor at E, draw a matching arc intersecting the given ray at F
Step 3: Set compass to chord length XY (anchor at X, pencil at Y)
Step 4: Without changing compass, anchor at F and draw an arc intersecting the first arc at G
Step 5: Draw ray EG. ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC
classifications.behavior: The first arc captures the 'opening' of the angle at a given radius. The chord XY measures how wide the angle is at that radius. Transferring both the arc and chord recreates the identical angular opening at the new vertex.

examples.explanation:
Given: ∠ABC and ray from E.

1) Compass on B, any radius. Draw arc across both sides → intersections X (on BA) and Y (on BC).

2) Same radius. Compass on E. Draw arc intersecting the given ray → intersection F.

3) Compass on X, adjust to Y. This captures chord length XY.

4) Same chord setting. Compass on F. Draw arc intersecting the first arc at E → intersection G.

5) Straightedge: draw ray EG. ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC.

image.alt: Five sequential diagrams showing each step: (1) Arc on original angle. (2) Matching arc from new vertex. (3) Measuring chord XY with compass. (4) Transferring chord to mark G. (5) Drawing final side. Arrows connect each step to the next.
image.caption: The five steps of copying an angle: arc on original, arc on new, capture chord, transfer chord, draw side.

image.alt: Complete construction diagram: Original angle ABC with arc and chord XY marked. New angle FEG with matching arc and chord FG. Both angles marked with congruence arcs. Compass settings illustrated: radius for arcs, chord length XY = FG.
image.caption: Angle Copy Construction: ∠FEG ≅ ∠ABC, constructed without measuring either angle.

sections.introduction: Geometric constructions are not just procedures to memorize—they are visual proofs. Each construction works because of fundamental geometric principles: the definition of a circle, congruent triangles, and the properties of radii and chords.

introduction_image.alt: Two diagrams showing the geometric reasoning: (1) Copying a segment: the arc is a circle; all points on the arc are the same distance from the center, so CD = AB (equal radii). (2) Copying an angle: triangles formed by the arcs and chords are congruent by SSS, so the corresponding angles are equal.
introduction_image.caption: Why constructions work: circles define equal distances; congruent triangles prove equal angles.

key_concept.definition: Segment copying works because all radii of a given circle are equal. When the compass captures AB and draws an arc from C, every point on that arc is distance AB from C. The intersection with the ray gives the unique point at that distance. Angle copying works by constructing two congruent triangles (SSS) with the vertex, arc intersections, and chord, proving the angles are equal.
key_concept.context: Understanding why constructions work transforms them from recipes into geometric reasoning. The compass creates circles (all points equidistant from the center), and the chord length on an arc determines the angle measure. Congruent triangles are the underlying proof.

image.alt: Left: Segment copy — the arc from C has radius = AB, so any point on it is AB from C. Right: Angle copy — triangles ΔBXY and ΔEFG have BX=EF (same radius), BY=EG (same radius), XY=FG (same chord). By SSS, triangles are congruent, so ∠B ≅ ∠E.
image.caption: Segment copy: equal radii. Angle copy: congruent triangles (SSS). Constructions are proofs.

classifications.value: The construction relies on the definition of a circle: all points on a circle are equidistant from the center.
characteristics: A compass set to radius AB draws a circle of radius AB
Every point on the circle is exactly distance AB from the center
The intersection of that circle with a ray gives the unique point at distance AB along that ray
Therefore, CD = AB—the segments are congruent because they are radii of equal circles
classifications.behavior: The compass captures length AB as a radius. When you draw an arc from C, you are drawing part of a circle with center C and radius AB. Any point on that arc is AB units from C. The ray intersection selects the point in the desired direction.

examples.process: The geometric logic of segment copying
examples.explanation:
The compass set to AB defines a radius r = AB.

The arc drawn from C is part of a circle with center C and radius r.

Every point on this arc is exactly r = AB units from C.

Point D is the intersection of this circle with the ray from C.

Therefore, CD = r = AB, and CD— ≅ AB—.

image.alt: Circle centered at C with radius equal to AB. The ray from C intersects the circle at D. Label: 'All points on this circle are AB units from C. D is the point on the ray at that distance.' Matching radii highlighted.
image.caption: Segment copy proof: CD is a radius of a circle with radius AB, so CD = AB.

classifications.value: The construction creates two congruent triangles (SSS), proving the corresponding angles are equal.
characteristics: Triangle formed by vertex B and arc intersections X, Y on original angle
Triangle formed by vertex E and arc intersections F, G on copied angle
BX = EF (same compass radius for base arcs)
BY = EG (same compass radius for base arcs)
XY = FG (same compass radius for chord)
By SSS, ΔBXY ≅ ΔEFG, so ∠B ≅ ∠E
classifications.behavior: The construction secretly builds two triangles with three pairs of equal sides. Since SSS guarantees triangle congruence, corresponding parts are equal—specifically, the angles at the vertices are equal. The construction is a physical SSS proof.

examples.process: The SSS proof hidden in the construction
examples.explanation:
First arc: BX = BY = radius r₁. Second arc: EF = EG = same radius r₁.

So BX = EF and BY = EG (first two sides equal).

Chord capture: XY = FG = captured length (third side equal).

By SSS: ΔBXY ≅ ΔEFG.

Corresponding parts of congruent triangles: ∠XBY ≅ ∠FEG, which is ∠ABC ≅ ∠FEG.

image.alt: Two triangles superimposed on the construction: ΔBXY in the original angle and ΔEFG in the copied angle. Corresponding sides marked: BX=EF (r₁), BY=EG (r₁), XY=FG (chord). SSS Congruence → ∠B ≅ ∠E.
image.caption: Angle copy proof: ΔBXY ≅ ΔEFG by SSS, so the angles are congruent.

image.alt: Unified diagram: Left half shows segment copy with circle and equal radii. Right half shows angle copy with the two congruent triangles highlighted in different colors. SSS marked on all three corresponding sides.
image.caption: Why constructions work: Segment copy uses equal radii. Angle copy uses congruent triangles (SSS).

sections.introduction: Geometric constructions require precision with the compass and straightedge. Small errors in compass handling, arc drawing, or step order can produce inaccurate results. Knowing the common pitfalls helps achieve clean, correct constructions.

key_concept.definition: Common construction errors include: changing the compass opening during transfer, drawing arcs too light or too small to intersect clearly, confusing the chord measurement with the arc radius, and using the straightedge to measure instead of only drawing lines.
key_concept.context: Constructions are judged by accuracy and clarity. A construction that works in theory can fail in practice if the compass slips, arcs don't intersect because they're too small, or steps are performed out of order.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Compass with a red X on the hinge — 'Don't change the opening'. (2) Two arcs that miss each other — 'Make arcs large enough to intersect'. (3) Confusion between arc radius and chord length — 'They are different measurements'. (4) Straightedge with forbidden ruler markings — 'Never measure with the straightedge'.
image.caption: Common construction errors: changed compass setting, arcs too small, confusing radius and chord, and measuring instead of constructing.

classifications.value: The most common error: the compass radius changes slightly when lifting or moving the compass.
characteristics: Error: Compass opening widens or narrows when picked up from the paper
Result: Copied segment is too long or too short; copied angle is distorted
Prevention: Hold the compass by the handle (top), never by the legs
classifications.behavior: Friction against the paper, pressing too hard, or bumping the legs can alter the setting. A changed radius means the construction loses its geometric validity—the transferred measurement is no longer equal to the original.

examples.process: What happens when the compass setting changes
examples.explanation:
If copying AB and the compass opens wider during transfer: CD > AB (too long).

If copying AB and the compass narrows: CD < AB (too short).

In angle copying, a changed radius between arcs means triangles are not congruent by SSS, so the angle is not accurately copied.

image.alt: Two attempts at segment copying: (1) Compass opened wider during transfer, resulting segment too long — marked with red X. (2) Compass setting preserved, correct congruent segment — green check. Hand holding compass correctly by handle shown.
image.caption: A changed compass setting destroys congruence. Hold by the handle, never the legs.

classifications.note: Solution: Always hold the compass by the handle (the top knob). Avoid touching the legs. After transferring, double-check by placing the compass back on the original segment to verify the setting hasn't changed.

classifications.value: Drawing arcs with too small a radius, so they don't reach the target line or don't intersect each other.
characteristics: Error: Arc radius too small to reach the ray in segment copying — no intersection point D
Error: Chord arc and base arc don't intersect in angle copying — no point G
Result: Cannot complete the construction
classifications.behavior: For segment copying, the arc from C must be large enough to cross the ray. For angle copying, the first arc must intersect both sides of the angle, and the chord arc must be large enough to intersect the base arc at the new vertex.

examples.explanation:
For segment copying: Make the compass opening large enough that the arc clearly crosses the ray.

For angle copying: The initial arc should cross both sides of the angle—choose a comfortable radius, not too small.

The chord arc from F must intersect the base arc—if it doesn't, increase the chord by re-measuring XY carefully.

image.alt: Two error diagrams: (1) Arc from C too small, doesn't reach the ray — no intersection. Corrected with larger arc. (2) Chord arc from F misses the base arc — no point G. Corrected with careful chord measurement. Both corrections shown with green arcs.
image.caption: Arcs must intersect to define points. Use a large enough radius and careful chord measurement.

classifications.note: Solution: Choose a radius that is clearly large enough. For the initial arc in angle copying, use a radius about half the length of the sides. For chords, ensure the compass captures the exact distance between the two arc intersections.

classifications.type: Confusing Arc Radius with Chord Length
classifications.value: In angle copying, using the arc radius instead of the chord length for the final arc.
characteristics: Error: Setting the compass to the same radius as the base arc for the chord transfer step
Correct: The final arc uses the chord length XY (distance between arc intersections), not the original arc radius
The chord XY is usually different from the radius BX
classifications.behavior: The angle copying construction uses TWO different compass settings: (1) the radius for the base arcs (BX = EF), and (2) the chord length (XY = FG). Using the radius for the chord step produces an incorrect angle.

examples.process: Two different compass measurements in angle copying
examples.explanation:
Setting 1 (base arc radius): Distance from vertex B to arc intersection X. Used twice—once on original, once on new vertex. This determines where the arcs are.

Setting 2 (chord length): Distance from X to Y along the arc. Used once—to mark point G on the new arc. This determines the angle opening.

These are different measurements! Confusing them is a critical error.

image.alt: Original angle with two compass settings highlighted: Setting 1 (red) = radius BX, Setting 2 (blue) = chord XY. At new vertex: Setting 1 used for base arc, Setting 2 used for chord. Warning: 'Setting 1 ≠ Setting 2. Do not mix them up.'
image.caption: Two different compass settings: radius for the arcs, chord for the angle opening.

classifications.note: Solution: After drawing the base arc on the original angle, you have TWO distances available: radius (vertex to arc) and chord (across the arc). The radius is used for both base arcs. The chord is used only for the final intersection. They are not the same.

classifications.type: Using the Straightedge as a Ruler
classifications.value: Measuring lengths or angles with the straightedge instead of constructing them with the compass.
characteristics: Error: Measuring AB with a ruler and marking that length on the new ray
Error: Measuring ∠ABC with a protractor and drawing that angle
Correct: The straightedge is ONLY for drawing lines. All measurement is done by the compass.
classifications.behavior: Using measuring tools defeats the purpose of a construction. Constructions prove that lengths and angles can be transferred using only circles and lines. Measuring also introduces inaccuracy from the ruler or protractor.

examples.explanation:
Allowed: 'I set my compass to capture AB and transferred it.'

Not allowed: 'AB is 3 cm, so I marked 3 cm on the new ray with my ruler.'

Allowed: 'I captured the chord XY and transferred it to construct the angle.'

Not allowed: 'The angle is 45°, so I used a protractor to draw 45°.'

image.alt: Two diagrams side by side: (1) Straightedge used as a ruler with markings — red X, labeled 'Not a construction'. (2) Compass capturing distance and transferring — green check, labeled 'True construction'. The contrast emphasizes the rule.
image.caption: A true construction never measures. The compass transfers; the straightedge only draws lines.

classifications.note: Solution: If you catch yourself reaching for a ruler or protractor, stop. Ask: 'Can the compass capture this?' The answer is always yes for these two constructions.

worked_examples.title: Diagnosing Construction Errors
worked_examples.problem:
A student attempts to copy angle ∠ABC. The resulting angle ∠FEG does NOT look congruent to ∠ABC. Below are possible errors. Identify which would cause this.

1) The student changed the compass opening between drawing the base arc on ∠ABC and drawing the base arc from E.

2) The student measured chord XY but then used that same setting for the base arc from E.

3) The student drew arcs that were too small and didn't intersect both sides of the angle.

step_by_step.explanation:
If the radius changed between drawing the arc on ∠ABC (radius r₁) and the arc from E (radius r₂ ≠ r₁), the triangles formed will not be congruent.

The sides BX and EF would not be equal → SSS fails → angles are not congruent.

Verdict: YES, this would cause the copied angle to be incorrect.

step_by_step.step: 2) Using chord length for base arc
step_by_step.explanation:
The chord length XY is generally different from the radius BX.

If the student used chord XY as the radius for the base arc from E, that arc would have the wrong radius.

The triangles would have mismatched sides → SSS fails.

Verdict: YES, confusing the two compass settings causes an incorrect angle.

If the arc from B doesn't intersect both sides, points X and Y are not defined → construction cannot proceed.

If the arc from E is too small, point F is not defined → construction cannot proceed.

This prevents completing the construction at all, rather than producing an incorrect angle.

Verdict: This would prevent construction, not produce a wrong-looking angle. The student would see the problem and adjust.

image.alt: Three panels: (1) Compass with two different radius settings, resulting triangles not congruent. (2) Compass set to chord XY used incorrectly for base arc — wrong radius. (3) Arc too small missing a side — construction incomplete. Each analyzed.
image.caption: Error analysis: changed settings and confused measurements cause incorrect angles.

worked_examples.final_answer: Error 1 (changed compass between base arcs) and Error 2 (using chord length for base arc) would both cause an incorrect copied angle. Error 3 would prevent completing the construction at all.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Angle copying relies on SSS congruence. Equal base arc radii and equal chords are both essential. Confusing the two compass settings or altering either one breaks the congruence.

sections.introduction: Geometric constructions are a hands-on skill. Practice the step sequences, understand the reasoning, and develop precision with your compass and straightedge. These foundational constructions appear in all subsequent construction topics.

worked_examples.title: Construction Practice Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Given segment PQ, construct a segment RS on a given ray such that RS = PQ.

2) Given ∠XYZ, construct an angle with vertex A and a given ray AB such that ∠CAB ≅ ∠XYZ.

3) Explain why the segment copying construction works in terms of circles.

4) In angle copying, what two compass settings are used, and why are they different?

step_by_step.step: 1) Copy segment PQ to ray starting at R
step_by_step.explanation:
1. Draw ray from R using straightedge.

2. Set compass: anchor on P, pencil on Q — this captures length PQ.

3. Anchor compass on R, draw arc intersecting the ray → point S.

4. Segment RS ≅ PQ. Both measured by same compass opening.

step_by_step.step: 2) Copy ∠XYZ to vertex A with ray AB
step_by_step.explanation:
1. Compass on Y, draw arc intersecting YX and YZ → points U and V.

2. Same radius, compass on A, draw arc intersecting AB → point B (on ray).

3. Set compass to chord UV (anchor on U, pencil on V).

4. Same chord, anchor on B, draw arc intersecting first arc → point C.

5. Draw ray AC. ∠CAB ≅ ∠XYZ.

The compass set to PQ defines a radius equal to PQ. When anchored at R, it draws a circle (or arc) of radius PQ. Every point on that arc is distance PQ from R. The intersection of this arc with the ray from R is the unique point S on that ray at distance PQ from R. Therefore RS = PQ.

step_by_step.step: 4) Two compass settings in angle copy
step_by_step.explanation:
Setting 1 (base arc radius): Distance from vertex to arc intersection. This determines the size of the triangles constructed. Must be the same for both the original and copied angle.

Setting 2 (chord length): Distance between the two arc intersections along the arc. This captures the angle opening at that radius. Transferred to mark the corresponding point on the new arc.

They are different because the chord length depends on both the radius AND the angle measure. For the same angle, a larger radius gives a longer chord.

image.alt: Four panels: (1) Completed segment copy PQ → RS with congruence marks. (2) Completed angle copy XYZ → CAB with congruence arcs. (3) Circle diagram explaining equal radii. (4) Diagram showing radius vs. chord, with two different radii producing different chord lengths for the same angle.
image.caption: Construction practice: performing copies and explaining the geometric reasoning.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Constructed RS ≅ PQ by capturing length with compass and transferring to ray.

2) Constructed ∠CAB ≅ ∠XYZ using two compass settings: base radius and chord length.

3) The arc from R is a circle of radius PQ; S lies on it, so RS = PQ (equal radii).

4) Setting 1: base arc radius (vertex to arc). Setting 2: chord length (across the arc). They differ because chord length = 2 × radius × sin(θ/2).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Segment copy: equal radii of circles. Angle copy: SSS triangle congruence via two compass settings.

summary: Basic geometric constructions use only a compass and straightedge to create precise figures without measurement. Key points: 1) Tools: The compass captures and transfers distances, draws circles and arcs. The straightedge draws straight lines only—no measuring is allowed. These tools implement Euclidean postulates: circles exist with any center and radius; unique lines exist through any two points. 2) Copying a Segment: Capture the segment length by placing compass points on its endpoints. Draw a ray from the new endpoint. With the same compass setting, draw an arc from the new endpoint intersecting the ray. The intersection defines the copied segment. Proof: All radii of a given circle are equal, so the copied length equals the original. 3) Copying an Angle: Draw an arc across both sides of the original angle. With the same radius, draw a matching arc from the new vertex. Capture the chord length between the arc intersections on the original angle. Transfer that chord to the new arc to mark the second side. Draw the ray. Proof: The construction creates two congruent triangles by SSS, so corresponding angles are equal. 4) Two Compass Settings: Angle copying uses two different settings—the base arc radius (vertex to arc) and the chord length (across the arc). These are different measurements and must not be confused. 5) Common Errors: Changing the compass opening during transfer (hold by the handle), drawing arcs too small to intersect, confusing the arc radius with the chord length, and using the straightedge as a measuring tool. 6) These constructions are the foundation for all more complex Euclidean constructions: bisecting segments and angles, constructing perpendicular and parallel lines, and building regular polygons.

Branches: Tools (compass captures/transfers distances, straightedge draws lines), Copy a Segment (capture length, transfer, mark intersection — equal radii proof), Copy an Angle (base arc, chord, transfer — SSS proof), Key Principles (circles define equal distances, SSS proves congruence), Common Errors (changing setting, small arcs, confusing radius/chord, measuring).
